proposals.title: The Formalization Continuum: How Gradations in Union Type Shape Parent-Child Ties and Father Involvement After Separation
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research on family dissolution often treats marriage and cohabitation as a simple dichotomy, despite evidence of growing diversity and legal ambiguity within cohabitation. This binary obscures how variations in relationship formalization, such as the presence of joint children, shared assets, or legal agreements, differentially structure parental roles and obligations. Understanding this continuum is crucial for predicting the long-term consequences of union dissolution for child well-being and parental support, especially for fathers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the degree of formalization of a parental union—measured by legal, social, and economic markers—is a stronger predictor of post-separation father involvement and parent-child relationship quality than the simple marital status at the time of birth. More formalized unions will be associated with more stable, supportive father-child ties after separation, even among never-married parents.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) and the OKiN survey, focusing on parents who have separated. We will construct a multi-dimensional 'formalization index' for each prior union, incorporating pre-2026 indicators: duration, presence of joint children, shared home ownership, and registered partnership status. Using structural equation modeling (SEM) and fixed-effects regression, we will test the effect of this index on post-separation outcomes (e.g., frequency of father-child contact, emotional support, child well-being), controlling for socio-economic factors and parental conflict.
proposals.contribution: This study moves beyond the marriage/cohabitation dichotomy to provide a nuanced, institutional theory of how union characteristics shape post-separation family dynamics. It will offer policymakers and family courts a more precise tool for understanding vulnerabilities in post-separation parent-child relationships. The findings will directly extend Kalmijn's work on weakened parent-child ties and the effects of father support by identifying the pre-dissolution roots of these outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares married and cohabiting parents, this proposal innovates by conceptualizing and measuring formalization as a continuous, multi-dimensional construct. It tests whether the mechanisms that institutionalize fatherhood operate along a gradient within both marital and non-marital unions, a non-obvious claim given the entrenched categorical approach in the literature.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity in Post-Divorce Vulnerability: Testing the Intersection of Family System Strength and Socioeconomic Resources Among Fathers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research consistently shows fathers are more vulnerable than mothers to well-being declines after divorce, particularly regarding parent-child ties and loneliness. However, it is unclear if this vulnerability is universal or conditioned by key societal and individual factors. This study addresses a critical gap by examining whether the strength of a country's family support system and a father's own socioeconomic resources buffer or exacerbate these negative effects.
proposals.hypothesis: The central hypothesis is that the heightened post-divorce vulnerability of fathers is not uniform; it is amplified in weak family systems (e.g., less normative familial support) but attenuated for fathers with high socioeconomic resources (e.g., education, income) due to greater capacity to maintain contact and well-being.
proposals.method: We will employ multilevel regression modeling on harmonized data from large-scale panel surveys (e.g., SHARE, GGS) across multiple European countries. The dependent variables are post-divorce father well-being (life satisfaction, depression) and parent-child contact frequency. Key independent variables are a country-level index of family system strength (derived from norms and policies) and individual-level socioeconomic resources. Interaction terms between gender, country-level strength, and individual resources will test the hypothesized heterogeneity. Analyses will control for time since divorce, repartnering, and pre-divorce characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This research will move beyond documenting an average 'father vulnerability' effect to delineating the conditions under which it is most acute. It will contribute to family sociology by integrating macro-level institutional and micro-level resource perspectives on post-divorce adjustment. The findings can inform targeted social policies aimed at supporting at-risk divorced fathers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work notes fathers' disadvantages, it rarely tests how institutional context moderates this pattern. Simultaneously examining the intersection of macro-level family systems and micro-level resources to explain variation in fathers' outcomes is a novel and crucial advancement.

proposals.title: Need versus Normative Obligation: Disentangling the Drivers of Material Support for Widowed Parents in Mediterranean Europe
proposals.motivation: Research on intergenerational support in Mediterranean family systems often emphasizes strong norms of filial obligation, but it rarely disentangles the effect of these norms from the effect of objective parental need. This is a critical gap, as understanding the true drivers of support is essential for predicting how family solidarity will evolve with changing demographics and welfare states. It also clarifies whether support acts primarily as a safety net or as a fulfillment of cultural scripts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while objective need (e.g., poor health, low income) is a primary driver of material support to widowed parents, perceived normative obligation significantly moderates this relationship, explaining regional and within-country variation in support patterns that need alone cannot account for.
proposals.method: We will analyze data from the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE), focusing on widowed parents aged 65+ in Italy, Spain, and Greece. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will specify a latent variable for 'perceived obligation' based on attitudinal survey items. The core analysis will employ a moderated mediation model to test if the effect of objective need (mediator: frequency of contact) on material support is conditional on the level of perceived obligation. Control variables will include children's resources, geographic distance, and sibling network characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, empirically tested model of support dynamics in strong-family cultures, moving beyond descriptive accounts of solidarity. It will contribute to theories of intergenerational exchange by formally integrating normative and need-based mechanisms. The findings will inform social policy on aging in Southern Europe by identifying the relative weight of cultural versus structural factors in family support.
proposals.novelty: The proposal innovatively operationalizes 'obligation' as a latent moderating variable rather than a presumed constant, allowing a direct statistical test of its role against need. It applies Kalmijn's expertise in parent-child ties and life course effects to the understudied context of widowhood in Mediterranean regimes, challenging simplistic cultural explanations.

proposals.title: Active Fatherhood and Kinship Mediation: A Quantitative Analysis of Men's Roles in Sustaining Family Networks After Divorce
proposals.motivation: The 'female-mediation model' in kinship studies, well-established in pre-2026 literature, posits that women are the primary maintainers of family ties. This model may be outdated, especially given rising father involvement and complex post-divorce family structures. Understanding the conditions under which men actively construct kinship networks is crucial for contemporary family sociology and for policies on child support, grandparenting, and the well-being of aging parents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant subset of divorced fathers actively mediate kinship ties between their children and the paternal extended family, and that this active mediation is positively associated with higher levels of instrumental and emotional support from the paternal kin network, as well as with fathers' own well-being.
proposals.method: We will analyze the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) and the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE), focusing on divorced fathers with non-resident children. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will test a mediation model where father-child relationship quality and father's own network activity predict paternal kin involvement. Latent class analysis (LCA) will be employed to identify distinct typologies of fathers' kinship mediation styles, and fixed-effects regression will assess the impact of these styles on longitudinal outcomes for child support and fathers' life satisfaction.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative test of the male-mediation hypothesis in a European context, directly challenging the gender essentialism of the female-mediation model. It will specify the demographic and relational conditions that foster active kinship work by men, contributing to more nuanced theories of post-divorce family dynamics and intergenerational solidarity.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kalmijn and others has examined weakened parent-child ties after divorce, this proposal uniquely shifts the focus to fathers as active agents who can strengthen, rather than merely weaken, kinship networks. It integrates the study of fatherhood, divorce, and intergenerational ties through a novel analytical lens of active network mediation.

proposals.title: Divorce and the Kin-Keeping Network: A Social Network Analysis of Post-Dissolution Kinship Structures and Child-Centered Ties
proposals.motivation: Prior research on divorce and family ties, including Kalmijn's work, often focuses on dyads (e.g., parent-child) or specific roles (e.g., fathers), neglecting the systemic restructuring of the entire kinship network. Understanding the multiplex connections between grandparents, aunts/uncles, and the non-residential parent is crucial, as this network provides critical support and affects child development and parental well-being. A structural analysis is needed to move beyond individual relationships to the holistic kin-keeping system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that marital dissolution leads to a systematic fragmentation and polarization of kinship networks, where ties become increasingly child-centered and cluster around the residential parent, weakening the structural integration of the non-residential parent's extended kin.
proposals.method: We will apply ego-centric Social Network Analysis (SNA) to kinship data from a large panel survey (e.g., the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study). For divorced parents, we will map alters (kin) and multiplex ties (emotional closeness, contact frequency, support exchange). We will compute standard SNA metrics (density, centrality, brokerage) and use community detection algorithms (e.g., the Louvain method) to identify clusters pre- and post-divorce. Statistical models will compare these structures to those of intact families, controlling for key covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, structural map of kinship network change after divorce, moving the field from dyadic to systemic analysis. It will identify which kin roles (e.g., paternal grandparents) are most vulnerable to detachment and how child-centered ties reconfigure network topology, offering new insights into intergenerational solidarity and inequality.
proposals.novelty: While SNA is common in friendship and professional studies, its systematic application to post-divorce kinship is novel. This approach quantifies the 'weakening' of parent-child ties in a broader structural context, revealing the network-level consequences that dyadic analyses miss.

proposals.title: Sequences of Union Dissolution and Re-formation in Later Life: A Sequence Analysis of Their Links to Parent-Child Support Exchanges
proposals.motivation: The life courses of older adults are increasingly characterized by complex partnership histories, including divorce, widowhood, and remarriage. Pre-2026 research, including Kalmijn's own work, shows these transitions significantly weaken parent-child ties and affect well-being, but the specific *ordering* and timing of these events is rarely studied holistically. Understanding how different sequences of partnership loss and re-formation shape patterns of intergenerational support is crucial for anticipating the needs of aging populations in societies with high divorce and remarriage rates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific sequences of later-life partnership transitions (e.g., divorce followed by long-term singlehood vs. rapid remarriage) are systematically associated with distinct, long-term patterns of support exchange (emotional, practical, financial) between older parents and their adult children. Sequences involving multiple disruptions will be linked to more attenuated support networks.
proposals.method: We will apply optimal matching analysis (OMA) and cluster analysis to retrospective life history data (e.g., from SHARE or national surveys) to identify typologies of sequences covering divorce, widowhood, and remarriage from age 40 onward. Using multinomial regression, these sequence types will then be used as the key independent variable to predict longitudinal outcomes of intergenerational support (giving and receiving), controlling for gender, number of children, and socio-economic resources. The analysis will leverage the `TraMineR` package in R for sequence visualization and clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study moves beyond studying single transitions to provide a holistic, dynamic view of how partnership biographies structure intergenerational solidarity in later life. It will identify which specific life course pathways are most consequential for support deficits, offering nuanced evidence for theories of family stress and cumulative disadvantage. The findings will inform social policy targeting vulnerable older adults with complex family histories.
proposals.novelty: While sequence analysis is established in life course research, its application to model the interplay between partnership histories and *dyadic* support outcomes with adult children is novel. This approach directly tests whether the *process* of family reconfiguration, not just its occurrence, determines long-term support patterns, integrating Kalmijn's expertise on divorce and support with advanced longitudinal methods.

proposals.title: Beyond the Single Perspective: A Dyadic Diary Study of Contact Quality and Well-Being in Divorced Parent-Adult Child Relationships
proposals.motivation: Research on the long-term effects of divorce on parent-child ties, a core theme in Kalmijn's work, often relies on retrospective or single-respondent reports of contact, which are prone to recall and perceptual bias. This limits our understanding of the dyadic reality of these relationships and their consequences for well-being, especially for older divorced parents. An objective, dyadic measurement of contact is crucial for unpacking the mechanisms linking weakened ties to outcomes like loneliness and support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that discrepancies in parent and adult child reports of contact frequency and quality, as captured by dyadic time-use diaries, will be a stronger predictor of lower subjective well-being for both parties than the absolute level of contact reported by either individual alone.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 divorced parent-adult child dyads in the Netherlands. Both members will complete a smartphone-based Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) diary for 14 consecutive days, reporting activities, co-presence (in-person, phone, digital), and concurrent mood at random prompts. We will employ multilevel Actor-Partner Interdependence Models (APIM) to analyze the dyadic data. Key analyses will focus on creating dyadic congruence scores (e.g., using polynomial regression with response surface analysis) to model the effects of reporting discrepancies on well-being.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, objective analysis of the dyadic nature of contact in post-divorce families, moving beyond static measures. It will directly test how perceptual alignment or misalignment within the dyad functions as a relational mechanism affecting well-being, thereby advancing theories on intergenerational solidarity and stress. The method offers a novel tool for life course and family sociology.
proposals.novelty: While time-use diaries exist, their application to the dyadic study of divorced parent-adult child relationships is novel. The innovation lies in using the discrepancy between matched dyadic reports as an independent variable, shifting focus from individual perceptions to the relational 'gap' as a key explanatory factor.

proposals.title: The Micro-Dynamics of Emotional Support: How Confiding and Emotional Exchange Mediate the Link Between Divorce and Adult Child-Parent Relationships
proposals.motivation: Prior research on post-divorce intergenerational ties, including Kalmijn's own work, has heavily focused on material support and contact frequency. However, the emotional core of these relationships—the exchange of confidences and emotional support—remains understudied, despite its known importance for well-being. This gap limits our understanding of how parental divorce fundamentally reshapes the intimate, relational fabric between parents and their adult children.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the negative association between parental divorce and adult child-parent relationship quality is significantly mediated by a reduction in reciprocal emotional support and confiding. Furthermore, we hypothesize that this mediation effect is stronger for father-child ties than for mother-child ties.
proposals.method: We will analyze data from the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) and the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE), focusing on adult children. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will test mediation models where parental divorce (and time since divorce) predicts emotional support exchange (measured by validated scales on confiding and emotional help), which in turn predicts relationship quality and well-being outcomes. Moderated mediation analyses will test for gender differences (parent/child) and life course stage effects using interaction terms within the SEM framework.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative evidence pinpointing emotional exchange as a key mechanism explaining strained post-divorce intergenerational ties. It will refine life course and solidarity-conflict theories by introducing a micro-level emotional dimension. The findings will offer nuanced insights for practitioners aiming to strengthen vulnerable family relationships, particularly father-child bonds.
proposals.novelty: While the link between divorce and weaker ties is established, this study uniquely shifts the explanatory focus from structural factors (contact, material aid) to the under-explored, micro-level process of emotional exchange. It directly tests whether the emotional support deficit is a primary pathway, especially for fathers, moving beyond descriptive accounts of relationship quality.

proposals.title: The Paradox of Strong Ties: How Dense Parent-Child Relationships Amplify Vulnerability to Social Isolation Following Late-Life Divorce
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on family ties, especially in later life, predominantly frames strong intergenerational relationships as a protective resource for health and well-being. However, this positive view overlooks a potential dark side: when a key family relationship dissolves (e.g., through divorce), individuals embedded in strong, exclusive ties may face greater risks of social isolation and diminished support. This is a critical gap, given rising divorce rates among older adults and the central role of adult children in parents' support networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that older divorced parents who reported exceptionally strong, multiplex ties with their adult children prior to divorce will experience a greater subsequent decline in social integration and well-being than those with weaker or more diversified pre-divorce networks, because the loss of the spousal tie more severely disrupts their primary, concentrated support system.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) and the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE), focusing on respondents who divorce after age 50. Using fixed-effects regression models, we will assess changes in loneliness, contact frequency, and received support. The key independent variable is a pre-divorce latent measure of 'tie strength' with children, constructed via confirmatory factor analysis from indicators of emotional closeness, contact frequency, and instrumental support. We will test for interaction effects between pre-divorce tie strength and post-divorce network diversification.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, dualistic theoretical framework for understanding family ties, challenging the uniformly positive 'strength' paradigm. It will offer concrete evidence on a specific life-course mechanism—late-life divorce—through which strong ties can become a vulnerability, informing policies aimed at preventing social isolation among aging populations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kalmijn and others shows weakened parent-child ties harm well-being post-divorce, this project flips the perspective to argue that very strong ties can also be detrimental under specific conditions, a counterintuitive claim not systematically tested in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Complementarity in Union: How Cultural and Religious Differences Can Strengthen Marital Stability and Parental Investment
proposals.motivation: Prevailing research on union dissolution, including Kalmijn's own work, often frames cultural and religious heterogamy as a risk factor for divorce due to value conflicts and reduced social support. This deficit perspective overlooks the potential for certain differences to create complementary resources or a synergistic division of roles that enhances stability, particularly in an era of increasing diversity and evolving gender norms. Understanding these protective mechanisms is crucial for a more nuanced theory of family dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific cultural or religious differences between partners, particularly those related to complementary gender-role ideologies or non-overlapping social networks, are associated with lower divorce risk and higher paternal investment, net of standard socioeconomic controls.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data (e.g., the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study or similar panels) using discrete-time event history models to predict divorce risk. Key independent variables will capture specific dimensions of difference (e.g., religiosity level, country of origin, gender-role attitudes). We will test for interaction effects between these differences and measures of complementary resources (e.g., specialized childcare, economic niche). Father involvement will be a secondary outcome, analyzed using fixed-effects regression to assess changes around the birth of children.
proposals.contribution: This study will challenge the dominant 'deficit model' of heterogamy by empirically identifying conditions under which differences are a source of strength. It will extend life-course and family stress theories by integrating the concept of complementarity. The findings will also inform debates on the consequences of immigration and secularization for family stability.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents the risks of heterogamy, it rarely tests for positive, stabilizing effects of specific differences. This proposal explicitly shifts the analytical focus from conflict to complementarity, linking cultural/religious dimensions directly to mechanisms of paternal support and role specialization.

proposals.title: Anticipating Marital Risk: How Perceived Divorce Likelihood Influences the Choice of a Foreign-National Partner
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Kalmijn's own work, has extensively examined how international marriages and migrant status affect divorce risk, treating nationality as an exogenous factor. However, this unidirectional focus overlooks a potential selection mechanism: individuals may incorporate their subjective risk of divorce into their partner choice. In an era of increasing transnational unions and growing awareness of divorce's consequences for children and parents, understanding this anticipatory behavior is crucial for a complete model of family formation.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals with a higher subjective assessment of their own future divorce risk are less likely to choose a foreign-national spouse, due to the anticipated complexities a potential divorce would introduce (e.g., international child custody, divided support networks, migration status uncertainties).
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) and the German Family Panel (pairfam), employing discrete-time event history models. The key independent variable is a time-varying measure of subjective divorce risk, constructed from survey items on relationship uncertainty and general attitudes towards divorce stability. The outcome is the transition into a marriage with a foreign-national vs. a native partner. We will use logistic regression with random effects to control for unobserved heterogeneity, and instrumental variable techniques (e.g., using parental divorce history and regional divorce rates as instruments) to address potential endogeneity between perceived risk and partner choice.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel causal perspective to migration and family sociology by modeling marital partner choice as a risk-averse strategy. It will expand theories of assortative mating to include anticipatory calculations about the potential dissolution of the union. The findings will also inform policies on transnational families by highlighting how institutional and legal frameworks around divorce may indirectly shape marriage markets.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard causality in the literature on international marriage and divorce, positing that divorce risk influences nationality choice in a partner, not just the reverse. It integrates Kalmijn's expertise on divorce consequences and intergenerational support with a forward-looking, agent-centric model of decision-making.

proposals.title: The Persistence of Intermarriage Effects on Parent-Child Relationships in an Era of Rising Diversity: A Cohort Analysis from 1995
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including seminal work by Matthijs Kalmijn, established that intermarriage (e.g., cross-national, interethnic) can weaken parent-child ties, potentially due to cultural distance and reduced intergenerational transmission. However, these findings are largely based on older cohorts who married in a less diverse societal context. It is crucial to test whether these effects persist for more recent cohorts, as societal norms around diversity have shifted and intermarriage rates have risen, which may have altered its familial consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the negative association between parental intermarriage and the strength of parent-child ties has attenuated for cohorts marrying from 1995 onward, as increased social acceptance and normalization of diverse unions reduce their disruptive effect on family dynamics.
proposals.method: We will replicate and extend Kalmijn's prior analyses using data from the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) and the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe (SHARE), focusing on cohorts who formed unions from 1995. The core analysis will employ multivariate regression and structural equation modeling (SEM) to estimate the effects of intermarriage (operationalized by partners' birth countries or ethnicities) on multiple dimensions of parent-child ties (contact frequency, emotional closeness, practical support). We will explicitly test for cohort-by-intermarriage interaction effects and control for key confounders like parental divorce, education, and geographical distance.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a crucial temporal test of a key theory in the sociology of the family, showing whether the well-documented 'costs' of intermarriage for intergenerational solidarity are a persistent feature or a historical artifact. It will directly inform debates on the integration of diverse families in aging societies and update our understanding of risk factors for weakened parent-child ties in later life.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established the effect, this study is novel in testing its temporal boundary conditions in the context of rapid demographic and normative change regarding diversity, moving from documenting a static association to analyzing a dynamic social process.

proposals.title: Spousal Embeddedness and Marital Stability: A Network Analysis of Shared Social Circles
proposals.motivation: Prior research on divorce, including Kalmijn's work on parent-child ties after divorce, highlights the importance of social integration for family outcomes. However, traditional survey measures of social support are limited in capturing the structural embeddedness of a couple within their broader community. Understanding how the overlap and structure of spouses' social networks influence marital stability remains a key gap, with implications for family policy and support systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that greater embeddedness of a marital dyad within a shared, cohesive social network—measured by network overlap, centrality, and community structure—is associated with lower risks of marital dissolution.
proposals.method: We will apply social network analysis to dyadic panel data (e.g., the Survey of the Social Networks of the Dutch). Using pre-2026 methods, we will construct ego networks for both spouses to measure overlap (Jaccard index) and joint centrality. We will employ community detection algorithms (e.g., the Louvain method) on the aggregated network to identify shared social circles and measure the couple's embeddedness within these cohesive subgroups. These network metrics will be used as predictors in discrete-time event history models of divorce.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, structural perspective on marital stability, moving beyond individual attributes to quantify the couple's position within a community. It will provide empirical evidence on how network cohesion acts as a constraint or support mechanism, directly extending Kalmijn's research on weakened family ties. The methodological integration of community detection with family demography offers a new tool for the field.
proposals.novelty: While network effects on individuals are studied, applying community detection algorithms to explicitly model the shared social structure of a couple as a predictor of dyadic stability is a novel synthesis of relational sociology and family demography.

proposals.title: The Long Shadow of Divorce: A 20-Year Longitudinal Study of Parent-Child Ties and Well-Being After Late-Life Union Dissolution
proposals.motivation: Prior research on divorce and parent-child relationships, including Kalmijn's own work, often relies on cross-sectional or short-term panel data, limiting understanding of long-term dynamics. The increasing prevalence of 'grey divorce' and the aging of populations in Western societies make it crucial to understand how separations in mid- and later-life affect family bonds and parental well-being over extended periods, beyond the immediate crisis phase.
proposals.hypothesis: The negative effects of divorce on parent-child relationship quality and parental well-being are not static but follow distinct longitudinal trajectories, with some relationships recovering or deteriorating only many years after the separation, and these patterns are moderated by factors like parental gender, repartnering, and geographic proximity to children.
proposals.method: We will utilize the long-running, multi-wave Dutch Kinship Panel Study (NKPS), which began in 2002, and link it to administrative registry data on marital status and household composition. Using fixed-effects panel regression models, we will analyze changes in relationship quality (contact, support, conflict) and parental well-being (loneliness, life satisfaction) for parents who divorce after age 50, tracing them for up to 20 years. Latent class growth analysis will be employed to identify distinct typologies of long-term adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, long-term picture of how parent-child ties evolve after late-life divorce, moving beyond snapshots to model dynamic processes. It will inform theories of family solidarity and life-course stress by identifying factors that promote resilience or lead to sustained decline in family relationships over decades.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has established short-term effects, this proposal uniquely leverages exceptionally long panel data to model trajectories of change over nearly two decades, capturing the full 'long shadow' of divorce and identifying late-emerging consequences for family ties that are invisible in shorter studies.

proposals.title: A Cross-National Dyadic Analysis: Replicating the Divorce-Child Well-being Model with Panel Data from Germany, the United Kingdom, and the United States
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Kalmijn's work, has established that parental divorce can negatively impact child well-being and parent-child ties, but findings are often based on single-country studies, primarily the Netherlands. In a pre-2026 context of rising divorce rates and diverse family policies across Western nations, it is crucial to test whether these complex intergenerational dynamics are universal or context-dependent. Replicating established models with comparable data from other major Western countries will clarify the generalizability of these life course effects.
proposals.hypothesis: The central hypothesis is that the dyadic, time-dependent associations between parental divorce, changes in father-child contact, and subsequent child well-being (e.g., internalizing problems, educational attainment) established in Dutch data will be replicated in panel data from Germany, the UK, and the US, but that the strength of these associations will be moderated by national differences in family law and social support systems.
proposals.method: We will conduct a direct replication of the dyadic Cox proportional hazards and fixed-effects panel models used in prior work. Using harmonized data from the German Family Panel (pairfam), the UK Household Longitudinal Study (Understanding Society), and the US Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID), we will model the hazard of relationship quality deterioration post-divorce for father-child dyads. The analysis will control for key pre-divorce characteristics (parental conflict, child age, socioeconomic status) and employ stratified models and interaction terms to test for cross-national moderation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, multi-country test of a leading dyadic model of post-divorce intergenerational relationships, moving beyond single-nation case studies. It will empirically assess the boundary conditions of existing theory by identifying which processes are robust across institutional contexts and which are attenuated or amplified by specific national settings, offering crucial evidence for policy debates on family support.
proposals.novelty: While cross-national comparisons of divorce outcomes exist, none have directly replicated a sophisticated dyadic life course model across multiple high-quality national panels with identical statistical specifications. The novelty lies in the rigorous, methodological replication focused on the dyad as the unit of analysis, explicitly testing for institutional moderation of micro-level family processes.

proposals.title: Cumulative and Interactive Risks: How Parental Divorce, Union Homogamy, and Father Involvement Jointly Predict Union Dissolution
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on union stability has identified key risk factors, such as parental divorce (intergenerational transmission) and heterogamy, but often examines them in isolation. Understanding how these factors combine and interact is crucial for a more realistic, person-centered model of divorce risk. This is especially relevant for policy and support services aiming to strengthen family resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: The risk of union dissolution is highest when multiple risk factors co-occur, specifically when individuals from divorced families form heterogamous unions and experience low levels of father involvement in childcare, with these factors exhibiting super-additive (interactive) effects.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) and/or the LISS panel, focusing on cohabiting and married couples with young children. Using discrete-time event history models, we will estimate the baseline hazard of separation. The core analysis involves introducing interaction terms between three key time-varying or time-constant covariates: parental divorce status, a multidimensional measure of union homogamy (education, religion, age), and a scale of paternal involvement in childcare and support. Model comparisons (likelihood-ratio tests) will determine if interactive models significantly improve fit over additive ones.
proposals.contribution: This study moves beyond main effects to provide a nuanced, configurational understanding of divorce risk, identifying high-risk profiles. It integrates Kalmijn's expertise on intergenerational ties and fatherhood into classical homogamy research. The findings will inform targeted family support programs by pinpointing where cumulative risks are most severe.
proposals.novelty: While homogamy and parental divorce are well-studied, their interaction with dynamic, gendered parenting behaviors—specifically father involvement—as a moderating or exacerbating factor is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of distinct research strands in family sociology.

proposals.title: Latent Trajectories of Marital Quality and Their Consequences for Parent-Child Ties and Well-Being in Later Life
proposals.motivation: Prior research on divorce and parent-child relationships often treats marital quality as static or uses simple linear models, obscuring heterogeneous long-term patterns. Understanding distinct trajectories of marital quality over the life course is crucial, as these patterns may differentially shape family dynamics, adult children's support, and the well-being of older parents, especially after divorce.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that latent class growth analysis will reveal distinct subgroups (e.g., stable high, declining, chronically low, post-divorce recovery) of marital quality trajectories. These trajectory classes will significantly predict later-life outcomes, with parents in declining or chronically low-quality classes experiencing weaker ties with adult children and lower psychological well-being.
proposals.method: We will apply latent class growth analysis (LCGA) to longitudinal marital quality data from the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) or similar panel surveys. Using Mplus software, we will model repeated measures of marital satisfaction to identify unobserved subgroups with distinct intercepts and slopes. The optimal number of classes will be determined via fit indices (BIC, aBIC, LMR-LRT). Subsequently, we will use multinomial logistic and linear regression to examine how class membership predicts parent-child contact, support exchange, and well-being (e.g., loneliness, life satisfaction), controlling for key covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study moves beyond static or average-effect models to provide a dynamic typology of marital quality development. It will clarify how long-term marital histories, not just divorce events, structure intergenerational relationships and aging outcomes, offering a more nuanced life-course perspective for family sociology and gerontology.
proposals.novelty: While LCGA is established, its application to model marital quality trajectories and link them specifically to later-life parent-child ties and parental well-being integrates Kalmijn's core themes in a novel, dynamic framework, bridging research on marriage, divorce, and intergenerational solidarity across the life course.

proposals.title: National Policy Contexts and the Intergenerational Consequences of Divorce: A Cross-Country Multilevel Study of Parent-Child Ties and Father Involvement
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Kalmijn and others shows that divorce weakens parent-child ties, particularly for fathers, with significant consequences for child development and parental well-being. However, the role of national-level institutional contexts in moderating these micro-level effects remains poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to design effective family policies to support vulnerable families post-divorce.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that more generous family policies (e.g., child support enforcement, parental leave, family allowances) and stronger norms of gender equality at the country level will attenuate the negative effects of divorce on father-child contact and relationship quality, and will buffer the associated decline in child and parental well-being.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multilevel analysis integrating individual/couple-level panel data from the Generations and Gender Programme (GGP) with national policy indicators from the OECD Family Database and the Comparative Family Policy Database. The analysis will use hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) for continuous outcomes (e.g., relationship quality, well-being) and multilevel logistic regression for binary outcomes (e.g., regular contact). Key independent variables will include divorce status (level 1) and country-level policy indices (level 2), with cross-level interaction terms to test the moderation hypothesis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, cross-nationally comparative evidence on how macro-level institutions shape the micro-level consequences of divorce for intergenerational solidarity. It will move the field beyond single-country studies, offering actionable insights for policymakers on which specific policy levers are most effective in supporting families through marital dissolution.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined either micro-level divorce effects or macro-level policy variation, this study's novelty lies in formally modeling their interaction using multilevel frameworks on harmonized cross-national data, directly testing how institutional contexts condition fundamental family processes.

proposals.title: Partnership Pathways and Parity: A Causal Analysis of Institutional and Normative Contexts on Fertility Among Highly Educated Women
proposals.motivation: Persistent fertility differentials by partnership status and education are well-documented, but correlations cannot distinguish selection into union types from the causal effect of union contexts themselves. This is a critical gap, as family policies (e.g., parental leave, childcare, tax benefits) often target specific partnership forms, assuming they causally influence fertility decisions. A pre-2026 understanding of whether partnership acts as a true 'gatekeeper' for educated women requires disentangling these processes across varying institutional settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the causal effect of partnership status (marriage vs. cohabitation) on first and second birth transitions for highly educated women is conditional on the institutional and normative context, being weaker in countries with strong support for gender equality and individual autonomy, and stronger where policies and norms privilege marriage.
proposals.method: We combine three methods. First, longitudinal event-history analysis (discrete-time logistic models) of harmonized panel data (e.g., GGS, PSID, GSOEP) will model selection into partnerships using time-varying covariates (education, income, attitudes). Second, a quasi-experimental difference-in-differences design will exploit sharp, exogenous variations in partnership-related policies (e.g., introduction of same-sex marriage, changes in cohabitation rights) as natural experiments to identify causal effects. Third, 60 in-depth interviews with highly educated women in contrasting policy regimes (e.g., Netherlands vs. Germany) will probe the normative and material mechanisms linking context, partnership, and fertility intentions.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a robust, multi-method test of causal mechanisms linking partnership and fertility, moving beyond descriptive associations. It will produce a contextually grounded theoretical model specifying the conditions under which partnership status is a consequential fertility gatekeeper. The findings will inform evidence-based family policy by clarifying which institutional levers most effectively support fertility goals across diverse family forms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of causal identification strategies from econometrics (policy shocks) with life-course modeling of selection and qualitative mechanism exploration, applied to the understudied group of highly educated women's fertility across different partnership trajectories.

proposals.title: Networked Norms and Life Course Sequencing: An Agent-Based Model of Gendered Influences on Education and Partnership Transitions
proposals.motivation: The link between educational attainment and family formation is a cornerstone of life course research, with persistent gender differences in sequencing (e.g., 'education first' for women). While often attributed to broad gender norms, the micro-social mechanisms—how norms are transmitted, enforced, and internalized within personal networks to influence individual decisions—remain a black box. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for explaining heterogeneity in outcomes across regions and cohorts, especially as family structures diversify and parental divorce influences intergenerational norm transmission.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed aggregate patterns in education-marriage sequencing emerge from the dynamic interplay of three micro-level processes: (1) the diffusion of normative expectations through kin and peer networks, (2) the localized enforcement of sanctions/rewards, which varies by network density and composition, and (3) the filtering of these social forces through institutional contexts like labor market opportunities and childcare policy.
proposals.method: We will integrate longitudinal panel data (e.g., Pairfam, NKPS) on individuals' networks, perceived norms, and life transitions with a purpose-built agent-based model (ABM). The ABM, implemented in NetLogo or Python (Mesa), will simulate a population of agents making sequential decisions on education and partner search. Agents will be endowed with mutable normative beliefs, influenced by network neighbors via a social influence algorithm (e.g., bounded confidence model) and by perceived costs/benefits of deviance. The model will be empirically calibrated and validated using the panel data, with key parameters estimated via approximate Bayesian computation (ABC).
proposals.contribution: This research will move the field from describing correlations to explicitly modeling the generative social processes behind life course heterogeneity. It will provide a formal, testable framework for how macro-level patterns in education and marriage are produced by micro-social interactions, offering new insights into the intergenerational consequences of divorce and changing father roles. The integrated empirical-ABM approach will serve as a template for studying other norm-driven demographic behaviors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of dynamic network influence, local norm enforcement, and institutional filters within a single computational model to explain life course sequencing, moving beyond static, individual-level analyses of norms. This directly addresses the call to model social mechanisms and leverages Kalmijn's expertise on intergenerational ties and family dynamics in a novel methodological direction.

proposals.title: Cultural Phylogenies and the Education-Marriage Link: Disentangling Historical Inheritance from Contemporary Adaptation in Europe
proposals.motivation: The link between women's education and marriage behavior (e.g., timing, prevalence) shows persistent regional variation across Europe, challenging modernization theories that predict uniform convergence. Pre-2026 research often treats countries as independent units, neglecting their shared historical and cultural ancestry. This project addresses this gap by formally modeling cultural relatedness to explain why some regions retain traditional patterns while others evolve more rapidly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the regional variation in the education-marriage link is not independent but reflects a phylogenetic signal, indicating inheritance from common cultural-historical origins (e.g., kinship systems, religious traditions). Regions sharing closer cultural ancestry will show more similar associations between education and marriage, even after controlling for contemporary socio-economic conditions.
proposals.method: We will construct cultural phylogenies for European NUTS-2 regions using linguistic families, historical state boundaries, and dominant religious traditions as proxies for shared ancestry. Using harmonized data from the European Social Survey and EU-SILC (pre-2026 waves), we will model marriage outcomes as a function of education, contemporary controls, and the phylogenetic structure. We will employ Phylogenetic Generalized Least Squares (PGLS) models and Bayesian phylogenetic methods (e.g., in R packages 'ape', 'brms', 'MCMCglmm') to estimate the phylogenetic signal (Pagel's λ, Blomberg's K) and partition variance between phylogenetic inertia and independent adaptation to modern pressures like female labor market access.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, historically-grounded framework for explaining demographic divergence, moving beyond static 'regime' typologies. It will formally test the role of cultural inheritance versus adaptation, offering a more dynamic understanding of family change. The methodological application of phylogenetic comparative methods to cultural-demographic questions represents a significant interdisciplinary advance.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic methods are established in biology, their application to model cultural relatedness and partition variance in demographic behavior is novel in family sociology. This approach directly challenges the assumption of unit independence in comparative studies and offers a rigorous test of historical path dependency versus convergence theories.

proposals.title: Beneath the Dual-Income Surface: An Ecological Succession Model of Gendered Role Stratification in Post-Marriage Households
proposals.motivation: Despite increases in female labor force participation and dual-earner households, pre-2026 research consistently shows a stalled gender revolution in the domestic sphere. This persistent inequality in housework and cognitive labor has significant implications for individual well-being, relationship stability, and intergenerational transmission of norms, yet its underlying, resilient structure remains inadequately explained by standard economic or attitudinal models.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying an ecological succession framework, we hypothesize that the post-marriage household represents a 'climax community' where surface-level economic equality conceals a deeply stratified and persistent structure of gendered domestic roles, maintained by legacy norms (the 'soil layer') and reinforced by life-course disturbances such as childbirth.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, sequential mixed-methods design. First, we will analyze time-diary and survey data from the Dutch LISS panel to model changes in domestic labor allocation before and after key disturbances (e.g., first birth). This will be supplemented with latent class trajectory analysis to identify stratification patterns. Second, we will conduct in-depth, biographic-narrative interviews with a stratified subsample to probe the 'soil layer' of cognitive labor, internalized norms from pre-marriage socialization, and negotiation processes.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel theoretical framework (ecological succession) to the study of household gender inequality, moving beyond snapshot comparisons. It will provide empirical evidence on how legacy norms stratify roles despite economic parity and offer a dynamic model for predicting how life events entrench or disrupt these hidden hierarchies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reconceptualizing the household as a stratified social ecosystem, using ecological models to explain the persistence of inequality. This moves beyond standard economic bargaining or time-availability theories by focusing on historical legacies and hidden layers of labor.

proposals.title: The Illusion of Parity: Uncovering Persistent Gender Asymmetries in Domestic Labor and Parental Support Following the Reversal of the Divorce Gap
proposals.motivation: Recent pre-2026 research documents a reversal in the 'divorce gap,' where divorced fathers' well-being now often equals or falls below that of divorced mothers, a shift sometimes interpreted as a sign of advancing gender equality. This interpretation is premature, as it may mask persistent, fundamental inequalities in domestic roles and parental investments that continue to structure post-divorce life. This study matters because it moves beyond aggregate well-being outcomes to test whether the underlying gendered organization of family life remains resilient, with significant implications for policy and theoretical models of family change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed convergence in post-divorce well-being between mothers and fathers masks a persistent gender asymmetry in domestic roles and parental support, where divorced mothers continue to bear a disproportionate burden of daily childcare and housework, while divorced fathers provide more intermittent, activity-based support, leading to different qualitative experiences of parenting and domestic life.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) and the LISS panel, focusing on divorced parents with minor children. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will construct latent variables for 'routine domestic burden' and 'instrumental/leisure-based parental support.' We will then employ a latent class analysis (LCA) to identify distinct typologies of post-divorce domestic organization and test their association with well-being outcomes (e.g., stress, loneliness) using fixed-effects regression models, controlling for pre-divorce characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a nuanced, multi-dimensional analysis of gender inequality in post-divorce families, moving beyond economic or global well-being measures. It will empirically test whether the reversal of the divorce gap signifies genuine role convergence or a new form of gendered inequality. The findings will inform theories of family stratification and provide evidence for targeted policies supporting both parents' involvement and well-being.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kalmijn and others has examined weakened parent-child ties and support effects, no study has systematically linked the reversal of the divorce gap to detailed, gendered patterns of domestic labor and childcare types. The novelty lies in treating the well-being convergence as a potential paradox to be explained, not an endpoint, using a combination of latent variable and latent class techniques to uncover hidden structures of inequality.

proposals.title: The Educational Threshold: An Agent-Based Model of the Phase Transition from Marital to Non-Marital Union Formation
proposals.motivation: The persistent rise of cohabitation and non-marital childbearing in Western societies, even in contexts with historically low non-marital fertility like the Netherlands, presents a puzzle for demographic transition theory. Understanding the systemic shift in union formation norms requires moving beyond individual-level correlates to model the dynamic interplay between individual agency and social influence. This research addresses a core question in family demography: under what conditions do new partnership norms become self-sustaining?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diffusion of non-marital union formation follows a phase transition, where a critical threshold in female educational attainment, interacting with local normative pressures, triggers a systemic shift from marriage-dominant to cohabitation-dominant regimes, even when fertility within these unions remains low.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a NetLogo or Python (Mesa) architecture. Agents (individuals) are embedded in a social network (e.g., small-world) and possess attributes (gender, education level, union status preference). Their union formation decisions are governed by a utility function balancing personal preference (influenced by education, acting as an external field) and social conformity (weighted by the proportion of married vs. cohabiting alters in their network, representing interaction strength). We will systematically vary the mean education level and network homophily to identify parameter regions (critical thresholds) where the system's dominant union type shifts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a formal, mechanistic model of normative change in partnership behavior, moving from description to simulation of the transition process. It will contribute to theories of the second demographic transition by specifying the conditions under which cohabitation becomes institutionalized. The findings will offer a novel framework for interpreting cross-national differences in union formation patterns based on educational distributions and social network structures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the formal logic of statistical physics (phase transitions, Ising-like models) to a core demographic process, explicitly modeling female education as an ordering field and social networks as the medium for normative interaction. This moves beyond standard regression analyses of individual-level data to capture emergent systemic properties.

proposals.title: Pathways of Protection: A Causal Mediation Analysis of the Positive Education Effect on Parent-Child Relationship Quality After Divorce
proposals.motivation: Recent sociological research has identified a 'positive education effect,' where higher-educated parents, including divorced fathers, often experience better outcomes in domains like well-being and family relationships. However, the specific social mechanisms driving this association remain a black box. Understanding these pathways is crucial for developing targeted social policies and support systems for families post-divorce. This study leverages pre-2026 advances in causal inference to move beyond correlation and quantify the processes at play.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the positive association between a father's educational attainment and the quality of his relationship with adult children after divorce is not direct, but is significantly mediated by two sequential pathways: (1) through higher post-divorce economic resources and stability, and (2) through the subsequent positive effect of these resources on the father's provision of practical and financial support to his adult children.
proposals.method: We will apply a formal causal mediation analysis framework, specifically a path model estimated using structural equation modeling (SEM) with maximum likelihood estimation. Using panel data (e.g., the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study or the Survey of Health, Ageing and Retirement in Europe), we specify education as the exposure, parent-child relationship quality (e.g., contact frequency, emotional closeness) as the outcome, and constructs for economic resources (income, wealth, housing stability) and instrumental support provision as sequential mediators. We will use the counterfactual-based mediation formulas (e.g., the mediation package in R) to decompose the total effect of education into natural direct and indirect effects, controlling for pre-divorce confounders like age at divorce and number of children.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first quantified decomposition of the mechanisms behind the education advantage in post-divorce father-child ties, distinguishing the role of economic capacity from the behavioral enactment of support. It will shift the literature from describing associations to explaining causal pathways. The findings will inform interventions aimed at strengthening vulnerable family relationships by identifying the most consequential leverage points.
proposals.novelty: While path analysis is common, the explicit application of modern causal mediation techniques from epidemiology to this family sociology question is novel. It moves beyond simply controlling for mediators to formally testing and quantifying their specific indirect effects, offering a more rigorous, mechanism-focused test of theoretical explanations for the education gradient in family life.

proposals.title: Pathways of Protection: Unpacking the Positive Association Between Parental Education and Child Well-Being After Divorce
proposals.motivation: Recent pre-2026 research has identified a reversal in the educational gradient of divorce effects, where children of highly educated parents now fare better post-divorce than children of less-educated parents. The mechanisms behind this new positive association remain poorly understood, limiting our ability to design effective support policies. This gap is critical, as understanding these pathways can inform interventions to mitigate the intergenerational consequences of union dissolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the protective effect of high parental education on children's post-divorce outcomes is primarily mediated by (a) higher levels of cooperative co-parenting and continued father involvement, and (b) greater economic and social resources that buffer children from stress.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) and the German Family Panel (pairfam), focusing on children experiencing parental divorce. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will test a multiple-mediator model. The model will specify parental education as the independent variable, child psychological well-being and educational attainment as outcomes, and pathways including post-divorce inter-parental conflict, frequency of father-child contact, instrumental and financial support, and the child's own educational expectations as mediators, controlling for pre-divorce characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond documenting the association to empirically identifying the specific behavioral and resource-based pathways that explain it. It will contribute to life course and family sociology by integrating literature on educational stratification, fatherhood, and divorce. The findings will provide evidence-based insights for family law and support services aiming to foster child adjustment after separation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kalmijn and others has established the changing correlation, this study is novel in its direct, comparative test of multiple theorized mechanisms—particularly the roles of co-parenting dynamics and sustained paternal support—within a unified causal modeling framework.

proposals.title: Causal Effects of Parental Divorce Sequences on Later-Life Well-Being: A Sequence Analysis and Matching Approach
proposals.motivation: Life course research has established that parental divorce is linked to negative long-term outcomes for children, but the causal mechanisms remain debated. Pre-2026 studies struggle to disentangle the effect of the divorce event itself from the confounding influence of pre-divorce family instability and post-divorce trajectories. This gap limits our understanding of how specific sequences of parental relationships causally shape adult children's lives.
proposals.hypothesis: The causal effect of parental divorce on adult children's well-being and own family formation is significantly moderated by the sequence of pre-divorce parental relationship quality and the timing and stability of post-divorce parenting arrangements. Negative effects are concentrated in trajectories involving high pre-divorce conflict followed by weak post-divorce father-child ties.
proposals.method: We will use holistic sequence analysis on longitudinal data (e.g., PSID, GSOEP) to cluster adult children's life courses from birth to adolescence based on parental union histories, conflict, and father involvement. Using optimal matching and multichannel sequence analysis, we will construct typologies of divorce trajectories. For each trajectory type, we will employ coarsened exact matching (CEM) or propensity score matching on the sequence-derived clusters to create balanced comparison groups, estimating the average treatment effect of the divorce sequence on outcomes like psychological well-being, partnership stability, and fertility transitions in adulthood.
proposals.contribution: This research moves beyond correlational descriptions by providing a formal causal framework for life course sequences. It will identify which specific divorce trajectories are most detrimental, offering targeted insights for policy. The integration of sequence analysis with causal inference sets a new methodological standard for studying life course mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While sequence analysis is descriptive and matching methods often focus on single events, this project innovatively uses entire prior sequences as the basis for matching, controlling for holistic life history as a confounder. This allows causal claims about the impact of specific transition points within a trajectory, a significant advance over existing methods.

proposals.title: Asymmetric Constraints and Joint Decisions: A Dyadic Analysis of Parental Support and Child Well-Being After Divorce
proposals.motivation: Research on post-divorce families often treats parents as a homogeneous unit or focuses on one parent's influence, obscuring how individual differences and power dynamics shape crucial joint outcomes like child support and co-parenting. Understanding these dyadic processes is vital, as they directly impact child well-being and parental life satisfaction, yet they are difficult to capture with standard regression models that assume symmetry and static interdependence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that post-divorce parental dyads are characterized by asymmetric actor and partner effects, where one parent's resources and constraints (e.g., new partnership, income) disproportionately influence joint decisions and child outcomes over time, and that these micro-level interdependencies explain macro-level patterns of support and child adjustment.
proposals.method: We will first apply the Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM) to longitudinal data (e.g., the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study) to estimate asymmetric effects of fathers' and mothers' characteristics on reported support and child well-being. Subsequently, we will develop an agent-based simulation (using a NetLogo-like architecture) where heterogeneous agents, endowed with empirically derived resources and preferences, interact under different bargaining rules (e.g., Nash bargaining, non-cooperative) to generate simulated distributions of support payments and child outcomes, which we will compare to observed panel data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, mechanism-based explanation for the persistence of inequality in post-divorce families by formally modeling dyadic bargaining. It will advance methodological integration in family sociology by combining statistical dyadic models with computational simulation, offering a template for studying other interdependent decisions. The findings will inform policies aimed at child support enforcement and co-parenting interventions.
proposals.novelty: The integration of longitudinal APIM with agent-based simulation to test explicit bargaining theories in family research is novel, moving beyond descriptive correlation to a generative, mechanism-focused approach. It directly applies a dyadic, dynamic framework to the understudied but critical context of ongoing parental relationships after divorce.

proposals.title: Dyadic Determinants of Divorce: A Couple-Level Analysis of Support, Conflict, and Parental Investment
proposals.motivation: Research on divorce and its effects on children has traditionally focused on individual-level factors (e.g., mother's income, father's involvement). However, family dynamics are fundamentally dyadic. Understanding the couple-level interactions—how partners' perceptions, support exchanges, and conflict patterns jointly predict marital stability—is crucial for a more complete picture of family dissolution and its intergenerational consequences, a core theme in Kalmijn's work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the risk of divorce is best predicted not by individual spouses' characteristics alone, but by the dyadic congruence in perceived support and the asymmetry in parental investment, particularly from the father's side, with greater asymmetry increasing divorce risk.
proposals.method: We will apply the Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (APIM) to dyadic longitudinal data (e.g., the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study). This statistical framework explicitly models the interdependence of partners' reports on relationship quality, support, and parenting. We will use structural equation modeling (SEM) to test for actor effects (one's own characteristics on one's own outcomes), partner effects, and interaction effects, specifically focusing on dyadic patterns in fathers' life course support and its effects on marital stability.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel, dyadic framework to the literature on divorce and parent-child ties, moving beyond individual-centric models. It will provide empirical evidence on how couple-level decision-making and matching in support roles shape family outcomes, thereby offering a more nuanced understanding of the mechanisms linking parental relationships to child well-being.
proposals.novelty: While APIM is established in social psychology, its application to the study of divorce determinants, particularly integrating pre-2026 themes of fathers' support and life course effects into a strict dyadic model of couple data, represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance in sociological family research.

proposals.title: Asymmetric Perceptions of Marital Quality and Divorce Risk: Consequences for Parental Investment and Child Well-being
proposals.motivation: Standard models of divorce and family dynamics often assume spouses share symmetric information and perceptions of marital quality. However, pre-2026 research on marital satisfaction and decision-making suggests systematic gender differences in perceptions and reporting. This asymmetry is crucial because differing perceptions of relationship stability may independently influence parental investments and co-parenting behaviors, with downstream effects on children, a core theme in Kalmijn's work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that greater asymmetry in spouses' perceptions of marital quality predicts lower and more conflictual parental investments (e.g., time and support from fathers) following divorce, which in turn mediates negative effects on child well-being.
proposals.method: We will analyze dyadic data from pre-2026 panel surveys (e.g., the German Family Panel, pairfam) where both spouses reported marital quality and expectations. Using latent class growth analysis, we will model trajectories of perceptual (a)symmetry over time for couples who later divorce. We will then employ structural equation modeling to test the link between these pre-divorce asymmetry classes and post-divorce measures of father involvement, inter-parental conflict, and child outcomes, controlling for socioeconomic factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a more nuanced, dyadic model of divorce precursors, moving beyond average marital quality to the role of disagreement itself. It will specify a mechanism—reduced and conflicted parental support—through which perceptual asymmetry harms children, directly extending Kalmijn's research on parent-child ties and well-being after divorce.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines marital quality as a predictor, this proposal uniquely treats the *discrepancy* between spouses' perceptions as the key explanatory variable for post-divorce parenting and child outcomes, integrating insights from family sociology and behavioral economics on asymmetric information.

proposals.title: The Role of Premarital Cohabitation in Gendered Divorce Initiation: A Longitudinal Analysis of Union Dynamics and Parental Investments
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research robustly established that premarital cohabitation is associated with higher divorce risk, but the mechanisms remain debated. Understanding who initiates divorce is crucial, as it relates to power dynamics, unmet expectations, and well-being outcomes for parents and children post-divorce, a core theme in Kalmijn's work. This study bridges the gap between union formation history and the often-gendered process of union dissolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that marriages preceded by cohabitation will see a higher likelihood of divorce initiation by women, particularly when the union involves children. This is because cohabitation may foster less traditional gender roles and different expectations, leading to a higher threshold for dissatisfaction among women when those expectations are not met within marriage.
proposals.method: We will analyze data from the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study (NKPS) and the German Family Panel (pairfam), employing discrete-time event history models. The dependent variable is the reported initiator of divorce (self or partner). Key independent variables are premarital cohabitation history, presence and age of children, and measures of relationship quality and gendered divisions of labor. We will use multinomial logistic regression to model initiation (by man, by woman, mutual) and employ mediation analysis to test if factors like perceived fairness in housework or father's involvement in child support (linking to Kalmijn's recent methods) explain the cohabitation-initiation link.
proposals.contribution: This research will move beyond simply linking cohabitation to divorce risk by elucidating the *process* of divorce, specifically who initiates it. It will contribute to theories of marital commitment and gender dynamics. The findings will inform policies aimed at supporting families, particularly children, by highlighting how early relationship transitions set the stage for later dissolution dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While the cohabitation-divorce link is well-studied, its connection to the gendered act of initiation is not. This proposal innovatively integrates Kalmijn's expertise on parent-child ties post-divorce and father involvement to examine if and how parental investments moderate the pathway from cohabitation to divorce initiation.

proposals.title: Testing the Gendered Framework of Divorce Initiation: A Comparative Study of Same-Sex and Different-Sex Marriages
proposals.motivation: The established literature on divorce in different-sex couples shows a persistent gender asymmetry in who initiates divorce, often linked to gendered roles and expectations. With the legalization of same-sex marriage in many countries pre-2026, a unique natural experiment exists to test whether these patterns are driven by gender per se or by the structural and relational dynamics within couples. Understanding this is crucial for refining family theory beyond a heteronormative framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the gendered pattern of divorce initiation observed in different-sex marriages will not be replicated in same-sex marriages. Instead, initiation patterns in same-sex couples will be more strongly associated with relational factors like the division of domestic labor, economic dependencies, and the presence of children, rather than the sex of the partners.
proposals.method: We will use longitudinal survey data from large-scale panel studies (e.g., the Netherlands Kinship Panel Study, US Panel Study of Income Dynamics) that include both same-sex and different-sex married couples. We will employ discrete-time event history analysis to model the hazard of divorce initiation. Key independent variables will include self-reported initiator status, detailed measures of pre-divorce household labor division, income ratios, and parental status. The analysis will compare coefficients for these predictors across couple-type subgroups, using interaction terms to formally test for differences.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly contribute to the literature on union dissolution by empirically testing the scope conditions of the gendered divorce framework. It will provide one of the first comparative, quantitative examinations of divorce dynamics in same-sex marriages, informing theories of marital stability. The findings will also have implications for understanding the well-being of children and parents in dissolving same-sex families, a key theme in Kalmijn's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined divorce risks for same-sex couples, a systematic, quantitative comparison of the *initiation* process using the established gendered framework from different-sex marriage research is novel.
